title: gvproxy cpu high on Mac M3
body:
### Issue Description

On a Mac M3, the gvproxy process exhibits high CPU usage after waking the system from sleep.
Stopping the Podman machine (podman machine stop) fixes the problem.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman machine start
2. running some container
3. mac sleep
4. wake 


### Describe the results you received

gvproxy cpu run on 400%

### Describe the results you expected

works fine

### podman info output

comments.body:
Which container are you running? I tried to reproduce the issue with the nginx container but was unsuccessful.

It's possible that something is initiating communication with the container after the system wakes up. It also appears you are not running the latest version of the Podman client, and the Podman machine is also outdated. Furthermore, your version of gvproxy might be old.

I suggest upgrading your components to the latest versions.

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

same here i have a `Apple M3 Pro`. and running on 5.6.2.
i cannot replicate this issue, maybe share the image you are using, also is this reproducible ?

I also have gvproxy use all my CPU. And this is before starting any container and without letting laptop to sleep. Apple M4 Pro.

> 
> It's possible that something is initiating communication with the container after the system wakes up. It also appears you are not running the latest version of the Podman client, and the Podman machine is also outdated. Furthermore, your version of gvproxy might be old.
> 
> I suggest upgrading your components to the latest versions.

Can someone run and provide output of this command when the issue occurs? `podman machine ssh -- 'sudo ss -tupn'`

> Can someone run and provide output of this command when the issue occurs? `podman machine ssh -- 'sudo ss -tupn'`出现问题时，能否请哪位运行一下这条命令并提供输出结果？ `podman machine ssh -- 'sudo ss -tupn'` Almost all the MBPs in our department have this problem, which is very bad.
PTAL @cfergeau @vyasgun @evidolob @guillaumerose

We are using company computers with security interception software that performs whitelist blocking on some podman requests, but it shouldn't affect the processes. I wonder if other people with the same issues as us have similar software on their computers. Could these external factors be causing the problem?

I wasn't able to reproduce this issue. I am using Podman v5.7.0 with gvproxy v0.8.6 on macOS 15.7.2 (24G325). I have last ideas on how to get more information about this.

Run `ps aux | grep /opt/podman/bin/gvproxy`

Locate the gvproxy log path (it should look like `-log-file /var/folders/49/<random-chars>/T/podman/gvproxy.log`) and share the content of these logs when you experience the issue.

I am not sure if this will provide any clues. It seems that a connection is lost when the MacBook is sleeping, and it retries to reconnect.

Alternatively, there could be a full buffer of requests on that socket that begins processing after wake-up or a garbage collection event. This would only apply if the high gvproxy usage lasts for a few minutes after waking. Has anyone measured how long the high usage lasts? Also, how long was the Podman machine running before sleep, and how long did it sleep?

I also wouldn't be surprised if new requests after wakeup are being rejected because the machine has a delayed internal time. And other apps/tools trying to reconect. If someone could investigate this theory: when the problem happens, run `podman machine ssh -- sudo hwclock -s`. If gvproxy usage decreases after this, you have probably found the issue.

I'm just brainstorming here. Feel free to suggest other theories.

According to this, it seems something is trying to connect to port 9092, which is closed. Does that port belong to a container?


> update: worked with IT and was able to get them to let me disable Zscaler temporarily... I restarted Podman, started containers, and did _not_ observe high CPU. Logged back in to Zscaler, restarted Podman, started containers, took 1 minute for 10 cores to be 100% consumed by gvproxy.

If Zscaler is trying to communicate with the container, gvproxy will use CPU regardless of whether the connection is successful. If it attempts multiple connections to sync, gvproxy has to process all that traffic.

Maybe sleep mode somehow closed the open port or something like that. This might have caused the container to go out of sync, leading to higher gvproxy utilization because it is receiving a flood of requests on closed ports. Similar to a DDoS attack.

When the 100% CPU consumption appears, are there noticeable network connectivity issues in the containers or in the podman machine? Is `podman ssh curl …` working fine for miscellaneous URLs?
Do you have any idea the kind of traffic zscaler would be blocking in this case?

I have never measured this. I've waited a few minutes then given up and gone off to do something else. I'm not entirely sure that it is only after a sleep. I'll try get more information about this.

This also didn't help although it is interesting that it was port 123.

Here is what Claude Code found:

```
⏺ Excellent! I found the root cause of the high CPU usage. Let me count the actual number of UDP connections:

⏺ Excellent! I've found the root cause. Let me check one more thing to see how many total UDP connections there are:

⏺ Bash(lsof -p 10934 | wc -l)
  ⎿  371

⏺ Bash(lsof -p 10934 -i UDP | grep ntp | wc -l)
  ⎿  337

⏺ Root Cause Analysis

  I've identified the issue with your gvproxy process spinning at 1200% CPU! Here's what I found:

  🔴 Current State:

  - CPU Usage: 1082.3% (consuming over 10 CPU cores)
  - Uptime: Over 8108 minutes (~5.6 days)
  - Open File Descriptors: 371 total
  - UDP NTP Connections: 337 leaked connections to NTP server (6.6.1.85:123)

  🎯 Root Cause:

  NTP Connection Leak - gvproxy is creating and leaking UDP connections to NTP (Network Time Protocol) servers without properly closing them. The process has
  accumulated 337 open UDP connections to the NTP server 6.6.1.85:123, which is causing the CPU to spin trying to manage all these connections.

  📋 Evidence from Logs:

  The log file shows repeated errors:
  Can't proxy a datagram to udp: dial udp 192.36.143.130:123: connect: network is unreachable
  Can't proxy a datagram to udp: write udp 10.10.10.10:54288->6.6.1.85:123: write: broken pipe
```

update: this was resolved for everyone on my team by the IT team undoing a change to zscaler around some kind of transparent proxy, so 100% caused by zscaler.

> update: this was resolved for everyone on my team by the IT team undoing a change to zscaler around some kind of transparent proxy, so 100% caused by zscaler.


Great, I'm happy to hear that. Also, I think the NTP issue is still relevant.

It could be related. Perhaps both zscaler and laptop sleeps are disturbing the NTP association in such a way that cause gvproxy to get into a bad state.

We have tons of stuff on our machines that could be contributing such as Threat Locker, Sentinel One, and now Global Secure Access (was Perimeter81). I wasn't seeing any blocks in them and when I disabled the VPN it still happens. Perhaps one of those plus sleep creates a perfect storm.

Let me know if there is something else to run to get more info. Like can I run gvproxy directly with extra flags or something?

I had the same issue running Podman behind Zscaler Internet Access. It's not ideal, but with Claude's help I disabled chronyd in the Podman machine and that fixed the gvproxy cpu issue.

```
podman machine ssh -- "sudo systemctl stop chronyd && sudo systemctl disable chronyd && sudo systemctl mask chronyd"
podman machine stop && podman machine start
```

With https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/commit/2d9e9313110e6d98ed044552958d51866e205802 in `gvproxy` main branch, we hope this issue is fixed. Testing welcome for those in this issue who are comfortable with building/replacing `gvproxy` on a machine where they can reproduce.

> podman machine stop && podman machine start
> ```

This change is part of [gvisor-tap-vsock 0.8.9](https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/releases/tag/v0.8.9) which was released today, this should ease the testing.

Thanks for the heads-up @cfergeau!  
I just grabbed a fresh podman machine and the darwin binary build of gvproxy 0.8.9 from the releases page and pointed podman to it using a custom `helper_binaries_dir` conf option.   

Seems to be working now, I tried several workloads and gvproxy does not get stuck in a busy loop anymore.  
Thanks for pinpointing the root cause of the issue and fixing it!

@MicheleSottocasa-Lexroom, can you please share more info on what you are running with podman, commands executed before, etc.?

> [@MicheleSottocasa-Lexroom](https://github.com/MicheleSottocasa-Lexroom), can you please share more info on what you are running with podman, commands executed before, etc.? Sure, after Mac goes to sleep, the CPU usage spikes. On podman, I'm running `docker.io/library/postgres` image, with `18.3-alpine3.23` tag Actually, I mitigate the issue by stopping the podman machine on Mac sleep

One of the theories when sleep is involved is that the machine gets behind on time, and then it tries to sync the time. It should be fixed in version 6. If that happens, try to verify the time on the machine with `podman machine ssh date`.

Another theory is that this is fixed in the latest gvisor-tap-vsock/gvproxy/release https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/27279#issuecomment-4421839537 but this needs to be confirmed.

> One of the theories when sleep is involved is that the machine gets behind on time, and then it tries to sync the time. It should be fixed in version 6. If that happens, try to verify the time on the machine with `podman machine ssh date`. I confirm, lost some seconds when Mac goes to sleep In the Image case, the shell reports the exact Mac time So maybe just by updating gvproxy to a >6 version I'll fix the issue?
